response:
## Deliberation

**What "decidable from a pilot" would require.** The pre-registered decision rule (Comment 1) is: *adopt Tiered if delta(Tiered − Sonnet-solo) ≥ −0.05 AND tokens(Tiered) ≤ 0.7× tokens(Sonnet-solo)*. Both halves of that inequality are computed from exactly two arms — **Tiered** and **Sonnet-solo**. Opus-solo and Haiku-solo don't appear in the rule at all; Opus-solo's role is explicitly secondary ("*Also report whether Tiered reaches Opus-solo quality at sub-Opus cost — the real prize*"), and Haiku-solo/Opus-solo's only pre-decision role is the 1-rep pre-screen used to pick the 8 tasks.

"Most expensive arm" unambiguously means **Opus-solo** (highest per-token cost; the issue itself labels it "quality ceiling" against Haiku's "cheap floor"). So the proposed shortcut is: run Opus-solo across the grid first, see if the gate is already decidable, and skip the rest if so.

**Steelmanning the pilot-first case.** The instinct has real merit in general sequential-testing practice — you don't want to spend on a 4-arm grid if 96 runs' worth of the priciest model already settles the question, and Comment 1 itself endorses *some* sequential economizing ("add reps/tasks... stop when unambiguous"). If the spirit of that clause is "spend the minimum to reach an unambiguous answer," piloting the costliest arm first looks like it's in that spirit.

**Where the steelman breaks.** It breaks on a structural fact, not a preference: Opus-solo data literally cannot resolve the adopt/reject inequality, because the inequality doesn't reference Opus-solo. No matter how many reps of Opus-solo you run, you learn nothing about delta(Tiered − Sonnet-solo) or tokens(Tiered)/tokens(Sonnet-solo). "Pilot the most expensive arm and stop early if the gate is decidable" is therefore not a real option — the gate is *never* decidable from that arm alone; the branch collapses to "run the full grid" every time. Proposing it as a genuine fork is a category error about what the pre-registered rule depends on.

A live version of the intuition — pilot the two arms the rule actually depends on (Tiered + Sonnet-solo) and treat Haiku-solo/Opus-solo as optional context — is more coherent, but it's a *different* design than what's pre-registered, and pre-registering-then-deviating is exactly the failure mode Comment 1 exists to prevent ("*Registered before running to avoid fishing for a favorable cut*"). Comment 1 pre-committed a specific sequential mechanism (escalate reps/tasks when a CI straddles the boundary) and did not pre-commit arm-level early stopping. Introducing a new stopping rule post hoc, after seeing partial results, is optional stopping — it inflates false-positive/false-negative risk on exactly the headline number (ADR 0019/0024's quality-per-cost framing depends on that number being clean). If arm-sequencing were worth doing, it needed to be specified *in* the pre-registration, with its own interim-analysis correction — not improvised at run time.

There's also a practical confound: staggering arms in time (Opus-solo now, others later-maybe) risks model/tooling drift between the pilot and the rest of the grid (grader version, task set, harness code), which the "same tasks, same conditions" design of a synchronized 4-arm run avoids. And the money at stake is modest and already accepted — Comment 3 sizes the whole test at ~250–300 invocations, and the owner's pre-spend gating list (grader independence, hermetic-control carryover, retry-cap format) doesn't include arm sequencing as an open question, i.e., the spend was already implicitly approved at that scale.

## Decision

**Execute the full pre-registered grid as specified in Comment 1/Comment 3** — all 4 arms (Haiku-solo, Sonnet-solo, Opus-solo, Tiered) × 8 pre-screened tasks × 3 reps, run together/synchronized, with the *already pre-registered* sequential escalation applying only to reps/tasks ("add reps/tasks only where a CI straddles the decision boundary; stop when unambiguous") — not to arms.

**Reject** "pilot the most expensive arm (Opus-solo) first and stop early if the gate is decidable."

## Rationale

1. **Structural**: the adopt/reject rule is a function of (Tiered, Sonnet-solo) only. Opus-solo data cannot make it decidable at any sample size, so the proposed branch is a non-option, not a cheaper path.
2. **Pre-registration integrity**: Comment 1 pre-committed a specific, narrower sequential mechanism (rep/task escalation) precisely to prevent fishing; adding an unregistered arm-level stopping rule after seeing interim results is optional stopping on the very number the test is designed to protect.
3. **No compensating benefit**: since point 1 means Opus-solo-only can never resolve the gate, there is no cost saved by piloting it — the full grid still has to run regardless of what Opus-solo shows, so "pilot first" only adds a serialization delay with no expected-cost reduction.
4. **Confound avoidance**: running all arms synchronized over the same task set/harness/grader version avoids time-drift confounds that a staggered pilot-then-backfill design would introduce.
5. **Spend already sized/accepted**: ~250–300 invocations is the already-communicated cost of the pre-registered design; the owner's open pre-spend questions (Comment 3) don't flag arm sequencing as unresolved.

**Note for future test design** (not actioned here, since it isn't what's pre-registered): if interim cost savings are wanted, the pre-registerable version is an interim check on **Tiered + Sonnet-solo only** (the two arms the rule depends on) with a pre-specified correction — not "pilot the priciest arm."
